SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Interface
CONTENT:
In the context of enterprise architecture, an Interface represents a logical connection between two IT Products. It models the way in which data is exchanged between these IT Products, including the direction, frequency, and type of information being transferred.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Initiative
CONTENT:
An Initiative represents a targeted, time-bound effort that drives change within the organization. It requires dedicated resources, impacts the enterprise architecture, and contributes directly to the achievement of one or more defined objectives. Initiatives serve as the operational link between strategic intent and execution, enabling measurable progress toward business goals.
subject: Initiative

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Objective
CONTENT:
An Objective Fact Sheet is used to represent a strategic goal and the transformations required to achieve it. It serves as a formalized anchor for planning, tracking, and aligning enterprise-wide initiatives with business strategy. Objectives provide structure and traceability for transformation programs and enable consistent communication across business and IT stakeholders. Summary
Summary
subject: Objective

All modeling must comply with the defined guidelines.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Characteristics of an IT Product
CONTENT:
• It bundles business functions in a logically and functionally bounded way to process data.
• It may include custom-developed elements such as application code, configuration data, macros, etc.
• It can be a third-party software product, used as SaaS, cloud-based, or on-premises.
• It may represent a bundle of technical components that together provide infrastructure or business platforms.
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Business Process
CONTENT:
A business process is a structured sequence of logically connected activities designed to achieve a specific business or operational goal. A process may be: • Part of a higher-level process (process hierarchy), • Composed of subprocesses, • Or used to implement other processes.
subject: Business_Process

subject: Initiative

• This ensures clear accountability, accurate reporting, and effective governance.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Introduction
CONTENT:
IT components are modeled to describe the technical architecture of an IT product. In addition to infrastructure, platform, and service components required by the IT product, there must be at least one IT component that represents the technical characteristics of the product—such as its version, vendor lifecycle, or manufacturer. IT components can only be created by an authorized group of users. Currently, this group consists of the administrators and the use
subject: IT_Component

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits
CONTENT:
From an enterprise architecture perspective, the IT Product is the central modeling object across all architecture layers. It enables: • Functional development planning • Portfolio and release planning • Centralized documentation of: o Security o Compliance o Data protection o Governance requirements The IT Product is the primary reference object for ensuring: • Compliance with the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) • Fulfillment of corporate due dili
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Characteristics of an IT Product
CONTENT:
• It bundles business functions in a logically and functionally bounded way to process data.
• It may include custom-developed elements such as application code, configuration data, macros, etc.
• It can be a third-party software product, used as SaaS, cloud-based, or on-premises.
• It may represent a bundle of technical components that together provide infrastructure or business platforms.
subject: Technical_Component_Bundle

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Rule: When to Create a Separate IT Product
CONTENT:
Even if the same software is used across departments (e.g., CATIA, MATLAB), a separate IT Product must be modeled if: • It is used in a different business or logical context • It processes different types of data, possibly with different classifications • It involves different organizational responsibilities • It runs on different infrastructures (e.g., cloud vs. on-prem, Windows vs. Linux)
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Interface and Impact Modeling
CONTENT:
• Focus on identifying actual interfaces and points of impact.
• In most cases, assigning only the directly affected IT products is sufficient.
• If IT Product fact sheets are properly maintained, indirect impacts (e.g. on business capabilities, IT components, or processes) can be inferred via the linked IT products.
subject: Process

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Specific Modelling Rules and Best Practices
CONTENT:
for a Business Capability Purpose of Business Capabilities in EAM A business capability serves as a stable, strategic abstraction of what an organization must be able to do to deliver value and fulfill its mission. In the context of Enterprise Architecture Management (EAM), business capabilities provide a structured, technology-neutral view of the enterprise’s functional needs and enable alignment between business and IT.
subject: Business_Capability

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: System Integration: LeanIX ↔ BIC
CONTENT:
• If an application has been assigned to a process step in BIC, this assignment is also synchronized to LeanIX Application Export from LeanIX to BIC • All active IT products in LeanIX are synchronized to BIC as applications. • These applications can then be assigned to process activities within BIC. This bidirectional integration ensures consistency between process modeling and enterprise architecture views.
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Initiative
CONTENT:
An Initiative represents a targeted, time-bound effort that drives change within the organization. It requires dedicated resources, impacts the enterprise architecture, and contributes directly to the achievement of one or more defined objectives. Initiatives serve as the operational link between strategic intent and execution, enabling measurable progress toward business goals.
subject: Initiative

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Responsibility at the Organizational Structure Level
CONTENT:
• Division • Main Department • Department • Subdepartment (lowest level) In LeanIX modeling, unless explicitly stated otherwise, responsibility must always be assigned to the lowest available level of the organizational structure.
• Ensure that the assigned unit reflects the actual operational responsibility.
• This ensures clear accountability, accurate reporting, and effective governance.
